titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Frances Anne Edgeworth, November 4, 1818
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Texas A & M University, University of Tennessee, Wake Forest University, Xavier University of Louisiana, Southern Methodist University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date: February 19, 2026

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of Bodleian Library
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Frances Anne Edgeworth, 1769-1865, , Nov r 4th1818

sourceDesc.bibl: In addition to our edited version, this letter was previously published in

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

div.p:
The newspapers have told you the
dreadful catastrophe - the death and
the manner of the death of that great
and good man - Sir Samuel Romilly

My dearest mother there seems
no end of horrible calamities -
- there is no telling the how this
has been felt in this house - I did
not know till now that Mr. Dugald
Stewart had been so very intimate
with Sir Samuel and so much attached
to him - 40 years his friend! - and
now to lose him in this shocking man
ner at the moment most dangerous
for Mr. Stewarts own mind - Mr.
S - has been dreadfully shocked - he
has not been able to sleep these two
nights - he wanders about the house walking most feebly, leaning on his daughter
a melancholy spectacle - He was just
getting better - enjoyed seeing us - con=
versed with me quite happily the first
evening & the I felt reassured as to his
mind - But what may be the consequence
of this stroke none can tell - His amicable
daughter & Mrs. Stewart show great re-
solution and judgment - I rejoice that
we came - they say I am of use conversing
with him - The only thing to which he has
been able to turn his mind is the Ms
of my fathers life - He says that is very
interesting to him - This morning he
read all the preface and all the history
of childhood - His affectionate manner
of speaking of him & you at this time
is very touching - When he read the
account in the papers & Dumonts evi
dence and SirSamuels letter to Dumont
he cried bitterly and Mrs. S- said
that relieved him much - Lord
Lansdowne, did not sleep Lady L- says
and looks wretchedly - He was extreme
ly attached to Sir Samuel & besides pri
vate friendship as he says the public
loss is irreparable - Lord L- can hardly
speak on the subject without tears
notwithstanding all his efforts - Both

The first thought both of the Stewarts
and ourselves was to leave this house
to leave Lord & Lady L to themselves - But
Lord L to whom I spoke directly assured me
in the most affectionate manner that
instead of being any a constraint it was a
74 comfort to him & to Lady L to have people
with him who as he knows feel as
they do - How excessively Sir Samuel
R was beloved as well as expected
- Poor Dumont! - how unjust to think
he wanted feeling - His letters to Lord
L- during the time of Lady R's illness &
all his conduct to them show the
tenderest feeling.
Novr. 5th.
This morning Lord L has shewn me
a letter he hashe has just received from good Mr. Wish
-aw - Sir S on the 4th. Oct added a
codicil to his will appointing Mr. Waldegrave Lady
Lans Romilly's brother his executor
& guardian to his children - Lady R.
had formerly been sole guardian -
In case of this gentleman's death or
declining to act Sir Samuel appoints
Mr. Wishaw & Lord Lansdowne his
executors & guardians to his children
- Mr. Waldegrave he changed his name from Garbet for some estate, Lady Rs brother it is
tho cannot act he is in such bad health
- it is thought he will die -
It is evident from this codicil that
Sir Samuel at that time gave up hope of
Lady R - What resolution he shewed in
writing it - he has settled all his affairs
properly - It was feared From his calling for that
sheet of paper that & from his efforts to write it
was feared that he had left no will --

I know my dearest mother how all
I this will shock you - I know every thought
that will rush on your mind - for the
past & for the future ----

Mrs. D. S who from her knowledge
of H- guessed the truth with regard to W
from the moment she heard of a brain
fever -- she tells me she has had great
experience in two different families &
of intimate friends and has had the
advice of the first medical men Dr. Gregory
in particular - He was decidedly of opinion
that there was no chance of recovery for
a patient unless removed from their
friends & their home - He says they compare
their actual with their past state at home
& this with the worst effect -- But that
elsewhere they compare themselves with
other patients & have hope of cure & motive for
exerting themselves to get well & get home
again -- In short without my attempting to
detail the reasons Dr. G decidedly was of this
opinion & in two cases where his opinion
was not followed from the injudicious tenderness
of friends the consequences were fatal
-- once to the patient herself -- and once to
& sister - a girl of 15 who only from seeing
& hearing what passed with the patient went
raging mad - The best chance of preventing
any catastrophe such as we fear from despair
as Mrs. Stewart says the trusting to the watching of
persons habituated to such attendance - The
patient always finds means to deceive friends
75 The His having spoken on that subject and
having suggested that it might come to that
is Mrs. D. S assures me the best proof that it
never will - Those who saynever doit -
- She has had long experience for many
years in several cases -

As I know the course of your mind
perfectly my dearest mother I have an-
ticipated the painful thoughts which
will occur & have offered all that has
given me any relief or hope - you know
Mrs. Stewarts affection & discretion there
fore you will not have any fears from
of her speaking on the subject.

Tell Lovell, my dear brother Lovell
with that Mr. Stewart remembers him
with great affection - Maria Stewart
is a charming girl - But I don't mean
anything by naming her so immediately
after Lovell - My heart is too full of me
lancholy things at this moment even
look forward to the pleasures of maneuvering -

The Stewarts stay here till Monday -
- then they go to Dr. Millar's Exeter -
& look for a house for winter -

We have written to Anna to say
that we will go to her on Monday for
a few days - I think my dearest
motherthat after weighing all the reasons
pro & con, and after considering all the
chances of advantage to Honora and hearing
all her wishes and feelings I have
come to a decision that it is most prudent
as well as certainly most agreeable to us
both to return home instead of spending
the winter in England -

However I will wait a few days
longer before I write to make irrevocable
engagements for our way home - I will
wait for some letter Conn or packet of
yours which has not yet reached me &
in which perhaps there may be some
reasons or request from you which
ought to weigh with us

At all events we I will decide not
to make any movement towards re-
turning till I hear that W. is safe at
Tunbridge -

It is my present intention as soon as I hear
that he has set out on his journey to
write to Mrs. E Sneyd to offer ourselves
to her - My aunts have written to Honora
to say that as we refused Mrs. Sneyds first
invitation it is now my business topropose
76 a visit - So I shall - propose staying there
a fortnight - a weekanother fortnight
between Mrs. M Powys and the Hol-
lands - You can calculate the rest
as well as I can -

I believe I must give up going to
the Hopes tho it hurts my conscience
& my inclinations to so but it wd be
such an immense round & expense

Adieu some carriage of I know
not what visitors are just come & I
must be ready for dinner

I enclose a note from Tom B
which shews he is a good natured tho silent creature

I beg to have the pleasure of
subscribing for that dear little Francis to Library & newspapers - as to pocket
money I leave the pleasure justly to
his dear mother
Maria E

postscript.p:
memorandum for future
The people that have been here
young Mr. Smiths relations of Lord L's
Mr. & Mrs. Ogden - he clever - & rich
She - the strangest of beauties
Pray get me a piece of bog oak for turning
for Mr. J. Wilson - I have promised it -

Pray write to your fatherbrother William
& ask him to inquire if a portrait of Spenser
can be had at a certain Castle Saffron near
Corke - now a ruin - but in whh. there was the
only portrait known of Spenser except one
in some Scotchman's possession - Does Dr. B
know of any other I promised Lady Spenser to
ask -

I have never received your drawings - I have
written to Ward -
Mrs. D S tells me that Wilkie saw the
sketch she has of the Irish dance & was
delighted with it - did not know whose it
was - said it was admirable -

div.closer:
Calne November
fifth 1818
Mrs. Edgeworth
Dr Beaufort's
Edgeworthstown
Ireland
Lansdowne
Near Dunleer
